coredata.dc:title: A molecular imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on blue/orange MXene quantum dots for visual detection of histamine
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Tianyao
Wang, Yu
Zeng, You
Tian, Xingguo
Xu, Xiaoyan
coredata.dc:description: Histamine is a highly toxic biogenic amine in food, making its sensitive and rapid detection methods vital for the assurance of edible safety and human health. Here, we explored for the first time a smartphone-enabled ratiometric imprinted fluorescence sensor based on blue/orange MXene quantum dots (MQDs) for fluorescence and visual detection of histamine. A linear relationship between the concentration of histamine and the fluorescence response of the sensor was found in the range of 1–60 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 21.9 nM for fluorescence detection and 92.2 nM for visual detection. In addition, the method was validated for the detection of real samples with excellent recoveries from 96.52% to 105.32%. Therefore, this work greatly expands the application of MQDs in the fluorescence sensing field, as well as provides a visual strategy for in-situ detection of histamine in food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ti3C2 MXene
Quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorometry analysis
Molecular imprinting
Histamine
Visualization

Detection of histamine in real samples
Exploration of molecular recognition and detection mechanisms
Visual detection of histamine by smartphone
Molecular recognition and detection mechanism analysis
Quantitative detection of histamine in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of Ti3AlC2 before (A) and after (B) etching. (C) EDS images and elemental content of the material before (above) and after (below) etching. TEM image and histogram of particle size distribution of BMQDs (D, E) and OMQDs (F, G). TEM images of OMQDs@SiO2 (H), individual OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs (I) and molecularly imprinted layers on the surface of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs (J). AFM images of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs (K).

XRD images (A) and FT-IR spectrograms (B) of related materials. XPS images of BMQDs (C), OMQDs (D) and OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs (E). N2 adsorption-desorption curves (F) and surface pore size distributions (G) of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs and OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs. (H) UV–vis absorption spectra of BMQDs, OMQDs and OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs. Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of BMQDs (I), OMQDs (J), and OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs (K), inset is their image under 365 nm UV irradiation.

Color stereo fluorescence spectrograms and raw fluorescence spectrograms after addition of histamine to the OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs (A, B) and OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs (D, E) system. Linear fitting curves of the fluorescence ratios (I468/I550) of the OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs (C) and OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs (F) system to histamine. (G) Histogram of the fluorescence response of the detection system to histamine in the presence of interfering substances. (H) Histogram of the change in fluorescence ratio (I468/I550) when 10 parallel samples were measured consecutively by the detection system. (I) Plot of the change in fluorescence ratio (I468/I550) of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs during 30 days of storage at room temperature.

(A) Schematic of reading fluorescent color picture information using a smartphone. (B) Linear fit curve of fluorescence picture R/B values to histamine concentration. (C) CIE chromaticity plot of the fluorescence color of the sensing system as a function of histamine concentration.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of histamine and sensing systems. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of histamine and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs. (C) Schematic representation of the hydrogen bonding recognition sites between histamine and functional monomers. (D) Schematic diagram of the detection mechanism of the system based on the theory of frontal orbits. Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of the sensor before (E) and after (F) addition of histamine.
Schematic design of a dual-emission MXene quantum dot molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescent sensor and principles of fluorescence detection and visualization of detection
Real sample detection and spiked recovery experiments for histamine (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A molecular imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on blue/orange MXene quantum dots for visual detection of histamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangzhou Institute for Food Inspection, Guangzhou 510410, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangzhou Institute for Food Inspection, Guangzhou 510410, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: No.483, Wushan Road, Tianhe District, Guangzhou City, Guangdong Province, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Histamine is a highly toxic biogenic amine in food, making its sensitive and rapid detection methods vital for the assurance of edible safety and human health. Here, we explored for the first time a smartphone-enabled ratiometric imprinted fluorescence sensor based on blue/orange MXene quantum dots (MQDs) for fluorescence and visual detection of histamine. A linear relationship between the concentration of histamine and the fluorescence response of the sensor was found in the range of 1–60 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 21.9 nM for fluorescence detection and 92.2 nM for visual detection. In addition, the method was validated for the detection of real samples with excellent recoveries from 96.52% to 105.32%. Therefore, this work greatly expands the application of MQDs in the fluorescence sensing field, as well as provides a visual strategy for in-situ detection of histamine in food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Histamine, known as one of the most important biogenic amines, which is produced by the decarboxylation of histidine under the action of microorganisms. It's commonly induced in various foods such as meat, aquatic products, and fermented products. Improper storage and transportation of these foods can lead to the growth of microorganisms, the metabolism of which produces decarboxylase enzymes that convert histidine in the food to histamine. Excessive intake of histamine can lead to symptoms such as dizziness, vomiting, allergies, or even death (Colombo et al., 2018). The content of histamine has been regarded as a crucial indicator for the freshness and hygiene of food in manufacture, disposition, and transport. Therefore, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) have set the limit standard system for histamine in fresh foods at 50 mg kg−1 (DeBeeR et al., 2021). In this sense, the development of sensitive and efficient detection methods for monitoring histamine in food is of utmost importance for food safety and public health.

Current histamine detection methods primarily encompass capillary electrophoresis (Oguri et al., 1997), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Altieri et al., 2016), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Yang et al., 2021), electrochemical techniques (Xu et al., 2022), and others. However, these methods necessitate specialized operators, expensive equipment, and intricate pre-processing steps, posing challenges in meeting the requirements for rapid and real-time detection (Barik & Mohapatra, 2022). In recent years, fluorescence sensing analysis has garnered significant attention due to its merits, including rapid response, high sensitivity, and user-friendly operation. Over the years, various fluorescent probes including quantum dots (QDs), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), semiconductor quantum dots, and upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) have been employed for histamine detection (Cao et al., 2022; Li et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2024). However, some of these fluorescent probes are complicated to prepare and contain toxic metal ions or organic agents. In addition, most of these probes rely on single-wavelength emissions, which are susceptible to environmental and instrumental noise, concentration fluctuations, and excitation intensity variations, thereby limiting their application (Yan et al., 2015). In contrast, ratiometric fluorescence sensors, capable of capturing analyte-induced changes in the fluorescence intensity from two or more emission bands at different wavelengths, prove effective in suppressing the interference of factors unrelated to analysis and enhancing sensitivity and accuracy (Liu et al., 2014). Moreover, the use of ratiometric fluorescent probes facilitates a wide range of color variations, allowing for the intuitive visual identification of analytes through integration with compact devices such as smartphones. For instance, You et al (You et al., 2022) introduced a molecularly imprinted trichromatic emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the rapid visual detection of environmental contaminants. MXenes have emerged as one family of the most concerned 2D nanomaterials of the 21st century thanks to their metallic conductivity, substantial specific surface area, excellent hydrophilicity, and biocompatibility. MXene-derived quantum dots (MQDs) inherit the advantages of MXene and exhibit significant enhancements in photoluminescence and quantum yield (QY) due to quantum confinement and the defect-induced luminescence phenomenon. Since Zhi's group first reported the selective and sensitive detection of Zn2+ in 2017 (Xue et al., 2017), an increasing number of MQDs-based fluorescence sensing assays have been developed successively. Nevertheless, most of the currently reported MQDs exhibit blue fluorescence. To the best of our knowledge, there has been no report on the application of blue and orange MQDs to develop a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for visual quantification of histamine.

Integrating fluorescent probes with recognition elements such as aptamer, antibody and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) is an effective and popular way to improve the selectivity of sensors toward target analytes. In particular, MIPs, as a copolymer with a lock-key combination similar to the target molecule, have been called artificial antibodies. Compared with natural receptors, MIPs offer specific recognition ability, exceptional thermochemical stability, and cost-effectiveness, making them an emerging research focus of sensors (Lu & Xu, 2017). For instance, Wei et al (Wei et al., 2017) designed a molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescent probe MIP@rQDs@SiO2 for the selective and sensitive detection of the drug mitoxantrone in human serum, achieving a remarkably low detection limit of 0.067 μM. Dai et al (Dai et al., 2023) developed a molecularly imprinted proportional fluorescence sensor based on blue/red carbon QDs for the visual determination of thiamethoxam. Hence, combining molecular imprinting technology with ratiometric fluorescence sensing offers a comprehensive approach to overcome the shortcomings and limitations of single methods for the detection of histamine.

Inspired by these studies, herein, we facilely designed a novel molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescent sensor (OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs) based on blue/orange MQDs for the visible and fluorescent detection of histamine in food. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , blue-MQDs (BMQDs) were synthesized and then wrapped with a molecular imprinted layer (BMQDs@MIPs) via the sol-gel method, functioning as the external response signal. Simultaneously, orange-MQDs (OMQDs) emitting orange fluorescence were prepared and used as the internal reference signal. Upon the addition of histamine to the system and under the excitation of 380 nm UV light, the sensor exhibited a decrease in the blue fluorescence peak at 468 nm, while the orange fluorescence peak at 550 nm remained unchanged. Intriguingly, the sensing system displayed a distinct color change from blue to yellow under 365 nm UV light in the presence of different concentrations of histamine, which enabled visual detection of histamine. In addition, taking account of the requirements of on-site quantification in practical applications, a smartphone-based application was interfaced for image acquisition and red-green-blue (RGB) analysis. Therefore, the proposed sensing platform in this study not only offers a swift and effective method for monitoring histamine in food samples but also shows a bright future for the application of MQDs in the field of sensing analysis.

Ti3AlC2 (98%, 200 mesh), LiF, histamine standard, dopamine hydrochloride, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane, ethyl orthosilicate were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine, putrescine, and glycine were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company. Chromatographically pure methanol was obtained from Merck Company, Germany. Acetonitrile was acquired from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. All chemicals were used directly without further purification. Deionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) was obtained from Millipore Water Purification Systems (Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA).

The morphological structure and surface elemental composition of Ti3AlC2 and Ti3C2 were obtained by ZEISS EVO MA 15 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Oberkochen, Germany). The microstructures of BMQDs, OMQDs and MIPs were obtained by Talos F200S field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Hillsboro, USA). Surface morphology of MIPs obtained by Bruker Dimenson ICON atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Massachusetts, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured by a Bruker Vertex 70 FT-IR spectrometer (Saarbrücken, Germany). The crystalline phase of the material was determined by Bruker X-ray diffraction (XRD) D8 Advanced Instruments (Saarbrücken, Germany). The elemental composition of the materials was analyzed using Thermo Fisher X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Massachusetts, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by a Thermo Fisher Evolution 300 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Massachusetts, USA). Nitrogen adsorption desorption curves, specific surface area and pore size distribution of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs and OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs were obtained by Conta Autosorb-iQ specific surface and pore size distribution analyzer (Florida, USA). Fluorescence spectra were measured by a Shanghai Yidian 970CRT fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). Absolute quantum yields of BMQDs and OMQDs and fluorescence lifetimes of the detection system were determined using an Edinburgh Instruments FLS 980 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK).

The synthesis of BMQDs and Ti3C2 nanosheets was carried out following our previous work (Wang, Xiong, et al., 2023). In brief, 0.2 g of the Ti3C2 nanosheets were uniformly dispersed in 20.0 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 2 h. The mixture was transferred to 50.0 mL of Teflon reactor liner, 5.0 mL of EDA was added and put into a stainless-steel reactor and reacted at 150 °C for 6 h. The reaction mixture was centrifuged at 10, 000 rpm for 30 min, the precipitate was collected and dried under vacuum and then stored at 4 °C in refrigerator. The supernatant was filtered by 0.22 μm membrane and then transferred to a dialysis bag with a retention capacity of 1000 Da for 1 h. The dialyzed liquid was collected to obtain BMQDs stock solution, which was stored away from light.

OMQDs were synthesized hydrothermally by sonication of Ti3C2 nanosheets with mixed acids. Firstly, 0.2 g of Ti3C2 powder was added to the mixture of concentrated sulfuric acid (1 mL) and nitric acid (3 mL) and then ultrasonicated for 2 h. After uniform dispersion, 25 mL of deionized water was added and cooled to 25 °C, and its pH was adjusted to 7 by adding NaOH. Subsequently, the solution was centrifuged at 8, 000 rpm for 5 min and the precipitate was transferred to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor liner containing 20 mL of deionized water and 10 mL of methanol, followed by adding 0.2 g of o-phenylenediamine, and the reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 6 h. Finally, the solution in the reactor liner was centrifuged at 10, 000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was collected to obtain an orange OMQDs solution, which was subsequently stored at 4 °C protected from light.

Firstly, the OMQDs were coated with a silica layer using the StÖber method. Briefly, 1 mL of OMQDs solution was added to 10 mL of anhydrous ethanol and were dispersed by sonication, and 1 mL of ethyl orthosilicate was added dropwise under stirring for 30 min. After that, 200 μL of ammonia was introduced and stirred continuously at 25 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the synthesized solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min, and then washed once with ethanol and deionized water sequentially, and the precipitate was dried under vacuum at 40 °C for 6 h to obtain OMQDs@SiO2. Next, 20 mg of OMQDs@SiO2 and 1 mL of BMQDs were dispersed into 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol, then 20 mg of histamine and 150 μL of APTES were added and pre-polymerized by stirring for 1 h. After that, 260 μL of TEOS and 150 μL of ammonia were added and stirred at 25 °C for 24 h in the dark. Upon completion of the reaction, the template molecules were removed by ultrasonic centrifugation using ethanol-acetic acid (9:1, v/v). Afterwards, the precipitate was dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h. Finally, OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs were obtained by sieving after grinding and stored at 4 °C away from light. OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs were prepared in the same procedure without the addition of histamine.

The prepared OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs were dissolved into deionized water to a concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1, and its pH was adjusted to 7. 200 μL of the solution was taken into a 1 mL centrifuge tube, and mixed with 200 μL of histamine with different concentrations (0, 1, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 μM). After that, the samples were incubated in a shaker at 25 °C for 20 min. 200 μL of the samples were taken in a micro cuvette and the sensitivity of the fluorescence spectrophotometer was set as 3, and the slit width as 10 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra of the samples were tested in the range of 400–700 nm under the excitation of UV light at 380 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of histamine using a smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: 1 mL of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs solution (0.5 mg mL−1) was transferred to a 5 mL centrifuge tube and mixed with 1 mL of histamine with different concentrations (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 μM), and then was incubated in a shaker at 25 °C for 20 min. After transferring to a cuvette, a series of images were captured under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light. The RGB information in the image was acquired by the App Color Picker on the smartphone, and the R/B value was used as a parameter to establish a relationship with the histamine concentration, which could be used to analyze the histamine content in the sample. All images were taken in dark confined environments to eliminate the distraction of ambient light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of histamine in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the practical reliability of the method, spiked recovery experiments were performed on real samples and compared with HPLC method. Recovery rate was calculated as the following formula: recovery rate (%) = measured value/ (sample background value + added value) × 100%.

Three types of fish products, namely mackerel, Atlantic cod, and canned tuna, were purchased from a local market (Guangzhou, China). The samples were spiked to the final histamine concentrations of 0 μM, 25 μM, 50 μM, and were extracted by centrifugation using 5% trichloroacetic acid, followed by removing the fat using n-hexane and extraction using n-butanol/trichloromethane (1:1, v/v). After that, fluorescence and visual detection were directly performed. For samples for HPLC assays, derivatization was continued using 10 mg L−1 of dansyl chloride according to GB 5009.208–2016. All the samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane prior to HPLC detection. The HPLC peak positions of histamine and internal standards and the standard curves of histamine standards are shown in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of molecular recognition and detection mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para:
Adsorption kinetics and adsorption thermodynamics were studied for OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs. 500 μL of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs (0.5 mg mL−1) and OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs (0.5 mg mL−1) were added to 500 μL of histamine standard solution (100 μM), respectively, and were centrifuged at 5, 000 rpm for 10 min after different incubation time (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 min). Histamine in the supernatant was quantified using the HPLC method and the adsorption capacity (Q) was calculated according to the following eq. (1):
(1)
Q =
∆ C ∙ V ∙ M
m
where ΔC (mg L−1) represents the difference between the concentration of added histamine and the concentration of histamine in the supernatant, V (mL) represents the total volume of the reaction solution, m (g) is the mass of each of the OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs and OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs, and M is the molecular weight of histamine.

The adsorption thermodynamic experiments were performed by changing the conditions in the above steps, the incubation time was fixed to 20 min, different histamine addition concentrations (5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200 μM) were set and the rest of the steps were consistent with the adsorption kinetics. In this case, the formulas for the two kinetic models are described as follows.

Quasi-primary kinetic modeling equations:
(2)
ln
Q e
−
Q t
=
lnQ t
− Kt

Quasi-secondary kinetic modeling equations:
(3)
t
Q t
=
1
K
Q e
2
+
t
Q e
where Q
t
represents the adsorption capacity (mg kg−1) of the corresponding OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs at an incubation time of t ; Q
e is equilibrium adsorption capacity (mg kg−1) for OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs; K is adsorption rate constant; t : incubation time (min).

Equation for Langmuir's isothermal adsorption model:
(4)
C e
Q e
=
C e
Q
+
1
K b
Q
where C
e
represents concentration (mg L−1) at adsorption equilibrium; Q
e
is adsorption (mg kg−1) at equilibrium; K
b
is Langmuir adsorption constant; Q is saturated adsorption capacity (mg kg−1).
In addition, the possible mechanisms of the detection system were explored in terms of absorption spectroscopy, frontline orbital theory, and fluorescence lifetime, respectively.

In this study, a mild etching method involving concentrated hydrochloric acid and LiF was employed to synthesize Ti3C2 nanosheets in situ. From the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images in Fig. 1A and Fig. 1B, it can be seen that Ti3AlC2 with a smooth block was transformed into Ti3C2 with an accordion structure. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), the module attached to the SEM, was utilized to analyze the elemental composition of Ti3AlC2 surfaces pre- and post-etching, and as illustrated in Fig. 1C, the aluminum (Al) content decreased significantly from 16.64% to 0.62%, confirming the effective removal of Al layers and the successful synthesis of Ti3C2 nanosheets. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of BMQDs as well as the particle size distribution are shown in Fig. 1D and Fig. 1E. The BMQDs were uniformly dispersed, and obvious lattice stripes can be seen in the inset, and the average diameter of the BMQDs was calculated to be 3.1 ± 0.8 nm. The TEM images and particle size distributions of OMQDs are shown in Fig. 1F and Fig. 1G, and similarly, the average diameter of OMQDs was calculated from the lattice fringes to be 7.4 ± 0.6 nm. The particle size distribution of BMQDs and OMQDs was measured by the software Nano Measurer 1.2.5. Compared with BMQDs, OMQDs had a larger particle size because the fluorescence color of quantum dots was determined by their particle size. Generally speaking, the larger the particle size is, the more the fluorescence emission color shifts to red (Alivisatos, 1996). The absolute QY of BMQDs and OMQDs, determined through steady-state transient fluorescence spectroscopy, was 21.12% and 7.74%, respectively, and the results are shown in Fig. S2.

Before the preparation of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), OMQDs@SiO2 were synthesized, as shown in Fig. 1H, and the OMQDs were encapsulated within the SiO2 shell to ensure their stability and minimize the fluorescence interference from external conditions. As shown in Fig. 1I and Fig. 1J, the MIP particles presented an irregular shape, comprising aggregates of single SiO2 microspheres with sizes ranging approximately from 500 to 600 nm. The surface morphology of the OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs was observed using atomic force microscopy (AFM). As depicted in Fig. 1K and Fig. S3, the surface of the OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs showed a rough shape and displayed numerous microporous structures, which indicated the successful formation of molecularly imprinted layers that provided an abundance of binding sites for target adsorption.

The surface elemental composition, functional groups, and chemical bonding of quantum dots can provide valuable insights into their optical and chemical properties (Zhao et al., 2023). As shown in Fig. 2A, the diffraction peaks of Ti3C2 and MQDs located at 39.0° were attributed to the characteristic peaks of the MAX phase (104), and the peaks were significantly lower, which was attributed to the fact that the etched Ti3C2 nanosheets and the hydrothermally synthesized MQDs still preserve some structural features of the thin-layer MAX (Li et al., 2019). In addition, the broadening and slight angle shift of MXene's (002) and (004) peaks, underscored their accordion-like structures (Ai et al., 2021; Boota et al., 2016). The diffraction peak of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs located at 22.6° corresponded to the characteristic peak of SiO2, indicating the presence of a molecularly imprinted layer (Li et al., 2016). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of Ti3AlC2 and Ti3C2, BMQDs and OMQDs, OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs and OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs are presented in Fig. 2B. For OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs and OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs, the peaks at 442 cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetric bending vibration of Si-O-C (Wang et al., 2009), and the peaks at 1130 cm−1 represented the asymmetric telescoping vibration of Si-O-Si (Yilmaz et al., 2024). There was almost no difference between the two spectra, reflecting complete template molecule elution during the preparation process of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs, as evidenced by the UV–vis absorption spectra of histamine during the elution process in Fig. S4. The absorption peaks located at 586 cm−1 and 790 cm−1 corresponded to TiO, CH. The absorption peak at 1650 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CO, which was related to the carboxyl group on the surface of MQDs. These characteristic peaks related to MQDs all appeared in the spectra of BMQDs, OMQDs, and BMQDs@MIPs. The absorption peak at 3450 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of -OH. The peak at 3282 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of -NH, indicated that the surface of the MQDs was passivated by amino groups. As shown in Fig. 2C and Fig. 2D, the peaks of BMQDs and OMQDs located at the four positions of 281.3, 399.5, 467.0, and 531.3 eV corresponded to C 1 s, N 1 s, Ti 2p, and O 1 s, respectively (Mashtalir et al., 2013), whereas the peak of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs located at the position of 99.0 eV corresponded to Si 2p (Luo et al., 2024), confirming the successful modification of the molecularly imprinted layer. These experimental results were consistent with the previous reports (Wang, Zhu, et al., 2023).

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (Fig. 2F) for OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs and OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs exhibited characteristics of a type I isotherm, indicative of abundant microporous structures on the OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs surface (Xu et al., 2023), supporting the observations from Fig. 1K and Fig.S3.

The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs was recorded at 6.852 m2 g−1 (Fig. S5), enabling its maximal adsorption capacity of 64.057 cm3 g−1 (Fig. 2F), which markedly surpassed that of the OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs. Furthermore, Fig. 2G demonstrated that the mean surface pore sizes of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs and OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs were 3.057 nm and 3.056 nm, respectively.

In addition, the optical properties of BMQDs, OMQDs, and OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs were investigated. As shown in the UV–vis absorption spectra of the three materials (Fig. 2H), for BMQDs, there was an obvious absorption band at 260 nm and a shoulder band extension at 300 nm, which were consistent with the known reported absorption band of MQDs (Chen et al., 2019). For OMQDs, the two absorption peaks located at 275 and 430 nm originated from the π-π* and n-π* transitions of CC and CO (Yuan et al., 2021), respectively. For OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs, their absorption peaks exhibit a superposition of the absorption peaks of both BMQDs and OMQDs. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of BMQDs (Fig. 2I), OMQDs (Fig. 2J), and OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs (Fig. 2K) demonstrated the excellent fluorescence emission capability and wide emission range of the three materials. From their fluorescence pictures under 365 nm UV irradiation in the corresponding inset, BMQDs exhibiting bright blue emission and OMQDs exhibiting orange fluorescence can be observed. In contrast, OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs showed a light blue color with two fluorescence peaks at 468 nm and 550 nm, and the peak of OMQDs showed a blue shift, potentially because the larger fluorescence peak of BMQDs absorbed the fluorescence of OMQDs and shifted it toward blue.

To enhance the sensor's detection efficacy, key conditions impacting its performance were optimized. The optimization began with determining the excitation wavelength of BMQDs, serving as the external response signal, to maximize fluorescence emission. The fluorescence emission spectra of BMQDs were evaluated across 310–410 nm wavelengths. As depicted in Fig. S6, optimal fluorescence emission was observed at 380 nm, establishing this as the sensor's excitation wavelength. In addition, the influence of pH, incubation time, and OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs concentration on the sensor's fluorescence response was systematically investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, maximum quenching by histamine occurred after 20 min of incubation, and subsequently reached equilibrium. Under acidic conditions, the fluorescence emission of the sensor was weak, which was attributed to the fact that some groups present on the surface of the MQDs, such as -OH, -COOH, and -NH2, were subjected to pH-induced protonation, resulting in the limitation of the fluorescence emission ability of the quantum dots (Choudhury et al., 2017). OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs concentration critically affected the sensing performance. Insufficient OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs failed to adequately recognize target molecules, whereas excessive OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs impeded sensing efficiency. Therefore, optimal conditions were established at a 20-min incubation under 380 nm UV light, pH 7, and OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1 for the subsequent test and analysis.

The ratiometric fluorescence test for histamine was performed using OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs under optimal detection conditions, with a histamine concentration gradient set from 0 to 60 μM. As shown in Fig. 3A and Fig. 3B, a decrease in fluorescence peak intensity at 468 nm was observed with increasing histamine concentrations, while the intensity at 550 nm remained almost constant. The ratio of these intensities (I468/I550) was used to establish a linear relationship with histamine concentration. As shown in Fig. 3C, this ratio exhibited a strong linear correlation in the range of 1–60 μM (R2 = 0.995), corresponding to the equation y = −0.00721× + 1.54364. The limit of detection (LOD), based on 3 times the signal-to-noise ratio, was determined to be 21.9 nM, well below the limit of detection specified in the standard (50 mg kg−1). Additionally, as an important parameter for evaluating the molecular recognition ability of MIPs (Yang et al., 2023), the imprinting factor (IF) was measured by calculating the ratio of the slopes of the relationship curves of the OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs to the OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs system. Fig. 3D and Fig. 3E illustrated that the OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs also responded to histamine, but weakly, which might be due to the nonspecific recognition of histamine by the OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs. The linear equation of the OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs system depicted in Fig. 3F was y = −0.00183× + 1.45751 (R2 = 0.989) in the range of 10–60 μM. Thus, a high IF of 3.94 can be obtained, indicating the excellent recognition capability on OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs in identifying histamine.

The sensor's resistance to interference, reproducibility, and long-term stability underwent thorough evaluation. As shown in Fig. 3G, several common biogenic amines, structural analogs, and metal ions (dopamine, lysine, putrescine, tyramine, glycine, histidine, K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Ni2+) were selected as interfering factors to test the performance of the sensor. Remarkably, the sensor exhibited minimal fluorescence response to these interfering substances, with only histidine and Fe3+ causing slight quenching. Nevertheless, the introduction of histamine elicited a pronounced fluorescence response and the response was unaffected by the interfering factors, despite their concentrations being tenfold higher than that of histamine.

Thus, the sensor exhibited high selectivity for histamine and excellent immunity to interference. As shown in Fig. 3H, we added 50 μM histamine to the sensing system and measured 10 samples consecutively, with each sample measured three times in parallel, and the results showed that the fluorescence response of the sensor for histamine presented a good reproducibility with RSD = 1.56%. In addition, the fluorescence stability of the sensor was also measured, and the results are depicted in Fig. 3I, where an assessment over 30 days indicated a modest reduction in the ratiometric fluorescence I468/I550, maintaining 88.9% of its initial value. As shown in Fig. S8A, the BMQDs and OMQDs maintained their fluorescence emission ability after two hours of UV irradiation at 365 nm, demonstrating their resistance to photobleaching. Meanwhile, the precision of the sensors prepared from different batches was tested, and as shown in Fig. S8B, the fluorescence response of the sensing system for histamine remained essentially unchanged between batches. Collectively, these findings highlight the sensor's superior selectivity, reproducibility, and optical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of histamine by smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, visual detection of histamine was demonstrated using a smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor. As depicted in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence color of the sensor notably shifted with varying histamine concentrations, correlating well with the color trend shown in the CIE chromaticity coordinate plot (Fig. 4C). Fluorescent color pictures RGB data were obtained by mobile app Color Picker.

Moreover, by capturing images with a smartphone, the R/B value of the fluorescence images showed a robust linear relationship with histamine concentration (0–60 μM) (Fig. 4B). This relationship was defined by the equation y = 0.50076× + 0.00453 and a correlation coefficient of 0.993, leading to a visual LOD of 92.2 nM for histamine. These findings underscore the potential of this sensor for sensitive, efficient on-site histamine detection, combining ratiometric fluorescence sensing with simple, accessible smartphone technology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular recognition and detection mechanism analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Adsorption kinetics and adsorption thermodynamics are two important indicators for evaluating the recognition performance of molecularly imprinted polymers (Yang et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. S9 and Table S1, the adsorption amount of histamine molecules by OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs increased with the adsorption time. OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs exhibited superior adsorption performance compared to OMQDs@BMQDs@NIPs, with a rapid increase within the first 20 min, after which the adsorption process tended to reach equilibrium. At this equilibrium, the adsorption capacity of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs toward histamine was 0.525 mg kg−1. This phenomenon can be attributed to the initial adsorption phase, where histamine was adsorbed by the surface-specific imprinted cavities of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs. This surface adsorption facilitated rapid histamine uptake due to minimal mass-transfer resistance. Once histamine adsorption in the binding sites reached saturation, the mass-transfer resistance between histamine and OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs increased, leading to equilibrium. We applied quasi-primary and quasi-secondary kinetic models in the adsorption kinetic data of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs, yielding R2 values of 0.890 and 0.992, respectively. This suggested that MIPs' histamine adsorption was predominantly influenced by chemical factors, particularly hydrogen bonding between histamine and effective imprinted sites (Fig. 5C), with less contribution from physical adsorption (Guo et al., 2019). Furthermore, the Langmuir model, utilized in adsorption thermodynamics, indicated monomolecular adsorption of histamine on OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs. This was supported by the R2 value of 0.989 from the model's fit, as presented in Fig. S10. In summary, the adsorption of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs for histamine occurred primarily through monomolecular layer chemisorption.

In addition, the fluorescence sensing mechanism of the detection system was explored from several aspects, including UV–vis absorption spectra, frontline orbital theory, and fluorescence lifetime. Fluorescence quenching mechanisms, primarily dynamic and static quenching, underpin most sensing systems (Dong et al., 2024). Static quenching involves the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex between the target molecule and the fluorescent probe. Therefore, static quenching does not affect the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorescent probe, yet alters the UV–vis absorption spectra of the system (Wang et al., 2014). As shown in Fig. 5A, when histamine was added to the system, the system did not show new absorption peaks, only the intensity of the peaks differed. Fig. 5E and Fig. 5F showed that the fluorescence lifetime of the system changed before and after histamine's addition, suggesting dynamic rather than static quenching (Liu et al., 2017; Zu et al., 2017). Dynamic quenching includes fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and internal filtering effect (IFE) (Xie et al., 2024). The prerequisite for FRET and IFE to occur is the partial overlap between the absorption spectrum of the target molecule and the fluorescence emission spectrum of the fluorescent probe (Liang et al., 2016). As observed from Fig. 5B, histamine only had a narrow absorption peak at 210 nm, whereas the emission peak of the fluorescent probe was located after 460 nm, indicating the system's quenching mechanism did not involve FRET and IFE.

Frontline orbital theory can effectively elucidate the reason for the quenching of the fluorescence emission of the detection system (Berezin & Achilefu, 2010; Joye et al., 2015). As shown in Fig. 5D and Fig. S11, upon excitation of the fluorescent probe, electrons transition from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), producing unpaired electrons. When histamine bound to the fluorescent probe, the higher LUMO energy level of histamine facilitates the transfer of electrons from the LUMO of histamine to the LUMO of OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs. This inter-system electron transfer prevents the electrons in the fluorescent probe's LUMO from transitioning to the HOMO, resulting in the fluorescence quenching (Suo et al., 2024).

Moreover, a decrease in the fluorescence lifetime of the system occurred before and after the addition of histamine. In summary, the fluorescence response mechanism of the detection system was predominantly attributed to PET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of histamine in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability and reliability of the proposed method were validated using three different types of real food samples: mackerel, frozen Atlantic cod, and canned tuna. Histamine standard solutions at three concentration levels (0, 25, 50 μM) were spiked into these samples, and the accuracy of the method was concurrently verified using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The obtained recovery rates ranged from 96.52% to 105.32% (Table 1 ). Furthermore, the results for histamine detection through our method were in close agreement with those obtained via HPLC, as indicated by a t-test (P > 5%), demonstrating no significant difference between the two methods at a 95% confidence level. Table S2 shows the comparison of the OMQDs@BMQDs@MIPs-based fluorescent sensing method with other reported methods for histamine detection. The results of the spiked recovery experiments for the mackerel samples in Table S3 show that the visual determination of histamine in the samples using a smartphone was also excellent, with satisfactory recoveries and coefficients of variation. This method possesses the merits of wide linear range and low LOD, which is more suitable for on-site detection of trace histamine in complex matrices.

In this study, we successfully synthesized blue/orange MQDs and developed a molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescent sensing system for fluorescence and visual determination of histamine in food. The sensor operated on a PET mechanism, where the presence of histamine weakened the fluorescence of BMQDs while keeping the fluorescence of OMQDs constant. At the same time, the fluorescence color of the detection system changed, gradually moving from light blue to yellow. By integrating this color change with smartphone technology, we translated indistinct visual changes into quantifiable RGB values. Therefore, this study not only opens new avenues for MQDs in sensing and analysis but also offers a new practical strategy for monitoring histamine in food.

Tianyao Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yu Wang: Resources, Methodology. You Zeng: Resources, Methodology. Xingguo Tian: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xiaoyan Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Guangdong Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2023A1515010338).
